examples.title: Algorithm: Matrix Multiplication with Shared Memory Tiling
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the optimized matrix multiplication approach using shared memory tiling to achieve coalesced memory access and reduce global memory bandwidth
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the optimized matrix multiplication using shared memory tiling. Key optimizations: 1) Coalesced memory access - consecutive threads access consecutive memory locations in global memory; 2) Shared memory utilization - each block loads a tile into fast shared memory and reuses it, eliminating redundant global memory reads; 3) Thread collaboration - threads within a block work together to load tiles efficiently. The outer loop iterates over tiles, while the inner loop computes partial products using fast shared memory.

examples.title: Python: CUDA Matrix Multiplication with Numba
examples.description: Python implementation using Numba CUDA to demonstrate optimized matrix multiplication with shared memory tiling and coalesced memory access
examples.code:
"""
GPU Matrix Multiplication using Numba CUDA
Demonstrates shared memory tiling optimization for GPU performance
"""

from numba import cuda, jit
import numpy as np
import math

# Configure CUDA settings
threads_per_block = (16, 16)

@cuda.jit
def matrix_multiply_tiled(A, B, C, N):
 """
 Optimized matrix multiplication using shared memory tiling
 
 Each thread block computes a TILE_SIZE x TILE_SIZE sub-matrix of C
 Threads within a block collaborate to load tiles into shared memory
 """
 # Declare shared memory for tiles
 # Shared memory is fast on-chip memory per Streaming Multiprocessor
 tile_A = cuda.shared.array(shape=(16, 16), dtype=np.float32)
 tile_B = cuda.shared.array(shape=(16, 16), dtype=np.float32)
 
 # Calculate global row and column indices
 row = cuda.blockIdx.y * cuda.blockDim.y + cuda.threadIdx.y
 col = cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x + cuda.threadIdx.x
 
 # Thread position within the tile
 tx = cuda.threadIdx.x
 ty = cuda.threadIdx.y
 
 # Each thread accumulates the result for one element
 sum_val = 0.0
 
 # Number of tiles needed to cover matrix
 num_tiles = math.ceil(N / 16)
 
 # Loop over all tiles
 for tile_idx in range(num_tiles):
 # COALESCED LOAD: Load tile from matrix A
 # Threads in a warp access consecutive memory locations
 if row < N and (tile_idx * 16 + tx) < N:
 tile_A[ty][tx] = A[row, tile_idx * 16 + tx]
 else:
 tile_A[ty][tx] = 0.0
 
 # COALESCED LOAD: Load tile from matrix B
 if col < N and (tile_idx * 16 + ty) < N:
 tile_B[ty][tx] = B[tile_idx * 16 + ty, col]
 else:
 tile_B[ty][tx] = 0.0
 
 # Synchronize threads in block - ensure both tiles are loaded
 cuda.syncthreads()
 
 # Compute partial result using shared memory (fast access)
 # Shared memory provides ~100x faster access than global memory
 for k in range(16):
 if (tile_idx * 16 + k) < N:
 sum_val += tile_A[ty][k] * tile_B[k][tx]
 
 # Synchronize before loading next tile
 cuda.syncthreads()
 
 # Write final result to global memory
 if row < N and col < N:
 C[row, col] = sum_val


def main():
 """
 Main function to demonstrate GPU matrix multiplication
 """
 # Matrix dimensions
 N = 1024
 
 # Allocate host matrices
 A = np.random.rand(N, N).astype(np.float32)
 B = np.random.rand(N, N).astype(np.float32)
 C = np.zeros((N, N), dtype=np.float32)
 
 # Allocate device matrices
 d_A = cuda.to_device(A)
 d_B = cuda.to_device(B)
 d_C = cuda.device_array((N, N), dtype=np.float32)
 
 # Calculate grid dimensions
 blocks_per_grid_x = math.ceil(N / threads_per_block[0])
 blocks_per_grid_y = math.ceil(N / threads_per_block[1])
 blocks_per_grid = (blocks_per_grid_x, blocks_per_grid_y)
 
 print(f"Matrix size: {N}x{N}")
 print(f"Threads per block: {threads_per_block}")
 print(f"Blocks per grid: {blocks_per_grid}")
 
 # Execute kernel
 matrix_multiply_tiled[blocks_per_grid, threads_per_block](d_A, d_B, d_C, N)
 
 # Copy result back to host
 C = d_C.copy_to_host()
 
 # Verify result
 expected = np.dot(A, B)
 if np.allclose(C, expected, rtol=1e-5):
 print("✓ Matrix multiplication completed successfully!")
 else:
 print("✗ Error: Results do not match")


if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation uses Numba CUDA to demonstrate GPU matrix multiplication with shared memory tiling. Key performance features: 1) Shared memory tiles (tile_A, tile_B) store data on-chip for fast access; 2) Coalesced memory loads - threads in a warp access consecutive memory locations; 3) cuda.syncthreads() ensures proper synchronization between tile loads and computation; 4) The algorithm reduces global memory bandwidth by reusing data from shared memory. Time Complexity: O(N³/T) where T is the number of threads. Space Complexity: O(N²) for matrices plus O(1) per thread.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(N²) for matrices + O(16×16×2) shared memory per block

examples.title: C: CUDA Matrix Multiplication with Shared Memory Optimization
examples.description: C implementation using NVIDIA CUDA to demonstrate optimized matrix multiplication with coalesced memory access, shared memory tiling, and occupancy optimization
examples.explanation: This C/CUDA implementation demonstrates GPU performance optimization techniques: 1) Shared memory tiling - tileA and tileB cache data in fast on-chip memory, reducing global memory bandwidth by ~16x; 2) Coalesced memory access - threads in a warp access consecutive memory locations, maximizing memory controller efficiency; 3) __syncthreads() ensures proper synchronization within thread blocks; 4) Occupancy optimization - grid and block sizes are calculated to maximize active warps per SM. The naive version shows poor performance due to uncoalesced access and redundant global memory reads. Time Complexity: O(N³/TILE_SIZE²) with parallel execution across blocks. Space Complexity: O(N²) for matrices plus O(TILE_SIZE²) shared memory per block.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(N²) for matrices + O(TILE_SIZE²) shared memory per block

examples.title: Python: GPU Performance Metrics and Analysis
examples.description: Python code demonstrating how to measure and analyze GPU performance metrics including memory bandwidth, occupancy, warp efficiency, and latency hiding
examples.code:
"""
GPU Performance Analysis and Profiling
Demonstrates how to measure and analyze key GPU performance metrics:
- Memory bandwidth utilization
- Compute throughput
- Warp efficiency
- Occupancy analysis
"""

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda
import math

@cuda.jit
def vector_add_kernel(a, b, c, n):
 """Simple vector addition - baseline for performance analysis"""
 idx = cuda.grid(1)
 if idx < n:
 c[idx] = a[idx] + b[idx]

@cuda.jit
def memory_bound_kernel(data, output, n):
 """Memory-bound kernel - demonstrates bandwidth limitations"""
 idx = cuda.grid(1)
 if idx < n:
 # Multiple memory accesses per computation
 temp = data[idx]
 for i in range(10):
 temp = temp * 1.001 + data[idx] * 0.001
 output[idx] = temp

@cuda.jit
def compute_bound_kernel(a, b, c, n):
 """Compute-bound kernel - demonstrates arithmetic intensity"""
 idx = cuda.grid(1)
 if idx < n:
 # Heavy computation with minimal memory access
 temp = a[idx] + b[idx]
 for i in range(100):
 temp = math.sqrt(temp * temp + 1.0)
 temp = math.sin(temp) * math.cos(temp)
 c[idx] = temp

def analyze_gpu_performance():
 """
 Analyze GPU performance characteristics and bottlenecks
 """
 print("=" * 60)
 print("GPU PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS")
 print("=" * 60)
 
 # Check if GPU is available
 if not cuda.is_available():
 print("No CUDA GPU available")
 return
 
 device = cuda.get_current_device()
 print(f"\nDevice: {device.name}")
 
 # Get device properties
 props = cuda.runtime.runtime.get_device_properties(device.id)
 print(f"Compute Capability: {props[0]}.{props[1]}")
 print(f"Global Memory: {props[2] / 1024**3:.2f} GB")
 print(f"Memory Bandwidth: ~{props[4] / 1e9:.2f} GB/s")
 print(f"L2 Cache Size: {props[17] / 1024:.2f} KB")
 print(f"Max Threads per Block: {props[6]}")
 print(f"Max Blocks per Grid: {props[7]}")
 
 # Test configurations
 sizes = [1000000, 10000000, 100000000]
 
 print("\n" + "-" * 60)
 print("MEMORY BANDWIDTH ANALYSIS")
 print("-" * 60)
 
 for size in sizes:
 # Allocate arrays
 a = np.ones(size, dtype=np.float32)
 b = np.ones(size, dtype=np.float32)
 c = np.zeros(size, dtype=np.float32)
 
 # Transfer to device
 d_a = cuda.to_device(a)
 d_b = cuda.to_device(b)
 d_c = cuda.device_array(size, dtype=np.float32)
 
 # Configure kernel
 threads = 256
 blocks = (size + threads - 1) // threads
 
 # Warm-up run
 vector_add_kernel[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c, size)
 cuda.synchronize()
 
 # Timed run
 start = time.time()
 vector_add_kernel[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c, size)
 cuda.synchronize()
 elapsed = time.time() - start
 
 # Calculate metrics
 bytes_transferred = 3 * size * 4 # 3 arrays, 4 bytes each
 bandwidth_gbps = (bytes_transferred / elapsed) / 1e9
 
 print(f"Size: {size:>12,} elements | "
 f"Time: {elapsed*1000:>8.2f} ms | "
 f"Bandwidth: {bandwidth_gbps:>6.2f} GB/s")
 
 print("\n" + "-" * 60)
 print("COMPUTE VS MEMORY BOUND ANALYSIS")
 print("-" * 60)
 
 size = 10000000
 a = np.random.rand(size).astype(np.float32)
 b = np.random.rand(size).astype(np.float32)
 c = np.zeros(size, dtype=np.float32)
 
 d_a = cuda.to_device(a)
 d_b = cuda.to_device(b)
 d_c = cuda.device_array(size, dtype=np.float32)
 
 threads = 256
 blocks = (size + threads - 1) // threads
 
 # Memory-bound kernel
 d_output = cuda.device_array(size, dtype=np.float32)
 start = time.time()
 memory_bound_kernel[blocks, threads](d_a, d_output, size)
 cuda.synchronize()
 mem_time = time.time() - start
 
 # Compute-bound kernel
 start = time.time()
 compute_bound_kernel[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c, size)
 cuda.synchronize()
 compute_time = time.time() - start
 
 print(f"Memory-bound kernel: {mem_time*1000:.2f} ms")
 print(f"Compute-bound kernel: {compute_time*1000:.2f} ms")
 print(f"\nCompute/Memory ratio: {compute_time/mem_time:.2f}x")
 print("Note: Compute-bound kernels scale better with GPU frequency")
 
 print("\n" + "-" * 60)
 print("OCCUPANCY AND LATENCY HIDING ANALYSIS")
 print("-" * 60)
 
 # Test different thread block sizes
 block_sizes = [32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024]
 
 print(f"\n{'Block Size':>12} | {'Blocks':>8} | {'Time (ms)':>12}")
 print("-" * 40)
 
 for bs in block_sizes:
 blocks = (size + bs - 1) // bs
 
 d_c = cuda.device_array(size, dtype=np.float32)
 
 start = time.time()
 vector_add_kernel[blocks, bs](d_a, d_b, d_c, size)
 cuda.synchronize()
 elapsed = time.time() - start
 
 print(f"{bs:>12} | {blocks:>8} | {elapsed*1000:>12.2f}")
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("KEY GPU PERFORMANCE INSIGHTS:")
 print("=" * 60)
 print("1. Memory bandwidth is often the bottleneck")
 print("2. Occupancy affects latency hiding capability")
 print("3. Larger block sizes may hit register limits")
 print("4. Balance compute and memory access for efficiency")


if __name__ == "__main__":
 analyze_gpu_performance()
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates GPU performance analysis techniques: 1) Memory bandwidth measurement - calculates effective bandwidth by measuring data transfer and execution time; 2) Compute vs memory bound analysis - compares kernels with different arithmetic intensities; 3) Occupancy analysis - tests different block sizes to find optimal configuration; 4) Latency hiding demonstration - shows how thread count affects performance. The code helps understand why some kernels are memory-bound (limited by bandwidth) while others are compute-bound (limited by ALU capacity).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for each kernel with parallel execution
